one_line_takeaway: A TMLR-published survey (88 works reviewed, 65 empirically characterized) that reorganizes MLLM adversarial attacks by attacker objective -- integrity, safety/jailbreak, control/injection, poisoning/backdoor -- rather than by modality, and explicitly maps each attack family to shared architectural root causes (cross-modal misalignment, embedding-space exploitability, modality imbalance, inherited encoder weaknesses, instruction-following trust), finding the literature is heavily vision-text skewed (58/65 works) and access-agnostic (36/65 black-box).
key_finding: The paper's substantive claims are quantitative characterizations of the surveyed corpus rather than new attack/defense results. Among the 65 works given full empirical characterization: by primary adversarial objective, 20 target model integrity, 21 target safety and alignment, 14 target behavioral control/instruction-following, and 11 target training-time reliability (one work, Tao et al. 2025, double-counted across safety and poisoning due to dual objectives). By target modality, visual attack surfaces dominate heavily: 58 of 65 works involve image or video input, 9 involve audio, 4 specifically target video, and only 7 works operate on purely non-visual surfaces -- the authors conclude this reflects both the field's concentration on vision-text attacks and comparatively limited coverage of audio/video threats. By attacker knowledge, 36 works are black-box, 16 white-box, 4 gray-box, and 9 span mixed threat models. The paper's qualitative contribution is the argument, elaborated through the vulnerability-centric analysis in Section 4, that seemingly disparate attack families recur because they share a small set of root causes in MLLM architecture -- cross-modal misalignment, a shared embedding space that can be steered to create spurious cross-modal correspondences ('adversarial illusions'), imbalanced/asymmetric reliance on individual modalities (e.g. models shown to weight text more heavily than vision, or video's temporal distribution of adversarial cues evading defenses tuned for single images), inherited weaknesses from pre-trained unimodal encoders that propagate unmitigated into the fused pipeline, and instruction-following behavior itself being treated as authoritative regardless of which modality carries it -- rather than being independent phenomena requiring independent explanations.
core_contribution: A peer-reviewed (TMLR, 03/2026) survey of adversarial attacks on multimodal large language models (MLLMs) that (1) proposes a goal-driven taxonomy organizing attacks into four top-level families by primary adversarial objective -- Integrity Attacks, Safety/Jailbreak Attacks, Control & Injection Attacks, and Poisoning/Backdoor Attacks -- rather than by modality or application, each family further split into representative subtypes (e.g. Integrity -> Signal Perturbations, Discrete Triggers, Representation & Fusion Exploits; Safety -> Unimodal Surfaces, Multimodal Composite Jailbreaks, Universal Jailbreak Triggers; Control -> Prompt Injection, System Instruction Manipulation, Tool/Retrieval/Agentic Injection; Poisoning -> Data Poisoning, Backdoor Attacks, Fine-tuning/Adapter/Representation Poisoning); (2) cross-cuts this taxonomy with an orthogonal attacker-knowledge axis (white-box, gray-box, black-box); and (3) presents a vulnerability-centric analysis (Section 4) that explicitly links the four attack families to shared architectural/representational root causes -- cross-modal misalignment & integration failures, embedding-space weaknesses, over-reliance/imbalanced reliance on specific modalities, asymmetric vulnerability across modalities, exploitation of the instruction-following core, attention-mechanism exploitation, inherited vulnerabilities from pre-trained unimodal components, component-specific weaknesses (e.g. LoRA adapters), and data/training-induced vulnerabilities -- via mapping tables (Tables 2-5) tying each taxonomy category to the vulnerability drivers it exploits. Section 5 then surveys defense mechanisms organized by the same attack-family structure (perception-layer robustness, context/instruction-handling defenses, control-plane constraints for agents, training-time safeguards). The authors state the survey covers 88 unique works overall, of which 65 are given full empirical characterization in a consolidated appendix table (45 attack papers from the taxonomy plus 20 additional works from the threat-model/vulnerability discussion), and the remaining 23 inform the defenses section.
threat_model: Not a single threat model -- as a survey it inventories the threat models used across the surveyed literature. It formalizes the general MLLM adversarial setting mathematically: an MLLM F takes multimodal input X = {X1,...,Xn}, each modality encoded by Ei into Zi, fused by ffuse into Zjoint, then decoded by g into output Y. An attacker seeks perturbations delta = {delta_1,...,delta_n} with ||delta_i||_p <= epsilon_i producing an adversarial input that induces an undesired output, formalized as untargeted attacks (maximize loss vs. true output), targeted attacks (minimize loss vs. attacker-chosen target), and jailbreak/harmful-content attacks (maximize probability of harmful content in the output). Attacker knowledge is treated as an independent analytical axis: white-box (full access to architecture/parameters/gradients, sometimes training pipeline -- used for gradient-based PGD-style optimization and to characterize worst-case/upper-bound vulnerability), gray-box (partial access, e.g. to an open-source vision encoder or training-data distribution while the LLM/alignment layers/deployment stay unknown -- motivated by MLLMs' common composition of reusable open-source submodules plus proprietary components), and black-box (query-only input/output access, reflecting realistic API-based deployment; relies on transfer-based or query-based/surrogate methods). Reported distribution across the 65 empirically characterized works: 36 black-box, 16 white-box, 4 gray-box, 9 evaluated under mixed/multiple access levels.
limitations: The survey text (through the portion extracted, roughly pages 1-22 of a 37-page paper) explicitly foreshadows a dedicated Limitations section (Section 6) and a Broader Impact section (Section 7) that were not reached in the paper, so their specific content cannot be reported here. Structurally, the authors themselves flag two limitations within the material extracted: (1) the taxonomy forces each attack into a single primary-objective category by design, even though they explicitly acknowledge attacks are often multi-objective and that lower-level mechanisms/secondary effects overlap across categories -- this is an admitted simplification rather than an oversight discovered later; (2) coverage is skewed by the underlying literature itself toward vision-text attacks (58/65 works) with audio (9/65) and especially video (4/65) substantially under-represented, which the authors attribute to the field's research distribution rather than a gap in their search methodology, but which nonetheless limits how much the survey's vulnerability claims about audio/video modalities can be considered well-evidenced versus extrapolated from a small subset.

quantitative_results: 88 unique works substantively analyzed overall; 65 given full empirical characterization in the appendix table
By adversarial objective (of 65): 20 integrity, 21 safety/alignment, 14 control/instruction-following, 11 training-time reliability (1 work double-counted across safety and poisoning)
By modality (of 65): 58 involve image or video, 9 involve audio, 4 specifically target video, 7 operate on purely non-visual surfaces
By attacker knowledge (of 65): 36 black-box, 16 white-box, 4 gray-box, 9 mixed/multiple threat models
